reasoning.typography: Generic sans-serif with no character — split green/white headline feels gimmicky rather than systematic, body copy is standard fintech neutral
reasoning.hierarchy: Hero headline dominates adequately but splits focus with Franklin portrait and scattered logos below — no single clear action hierarchy
reasoning.distinctiveness: Financial services blue gradient + 'revolutionary' + 'fast, safe, smart' = could be any fintech API platform. The Franklin engraving is the only memorable element
reasoning.color: Blue-to-cyan gradient with green accent and mint section break shows some restraint, but the gradient itself is busy and the green CTAs are predictable fintech
reasoning.density: Comfortable but generic spacing — hero has breathing room but follows standard padding rhythm without spatial personality or content-driven variation
reasoning.voice: Structure is pure B2B SaaS stencil: hero value prop → logo trust bar → product feature → CTA. IA doesn't reflect what makes Plaid actually different beyond 'data connections'

winning_patterns: Franklin portrait engraving treatment creates actual visual memorability
Gradient background with line-art texture adds dimensionality beyond flat color
Green accent color usage is relatively disciplined in CTAs
anti_patterns: 'Revolutionary financial products' headline could be any API company circa 2020
Logo parade of customers (Monzo/Affirm/Robinhood) is generic trust signaling
Event promo card floating in hero competes for attention without clear priority
Generic dual-CTA pattern ('Talk to team' + 'Start building') lacks conviction
Blue gradient fintech aesthetic is category table stakes, not brand distinction
